My job is basically all things content quality, which can include workflow and pipeline work, as well as just close reading to understand the delta between where we want to be quality-wise and where we are, and then articulating what the solutions might be so that Eng can tackle them or we can put it into the product pipe.

So specifically at DeepGram, like the big workhorse was Aerox.
We used it for everything because it's so convenient, right?
Um, um, my mainly, uh, what I've done is, uh, a lot of the content I've worked with lived on Airtable and Airtable to Aerox.
use Zapier to sort of bridge everything.

But, uh.
The workflows that I had running were mainly two kinds.
One is from product, we get like a brief, and then we human, we manually do like the SEO stuff, we do the outlining, do the keyword research, everything, we put all of that stuff lived on Airtable.
And we use triggers to do like the research with Airtable, we do the outlining, sorry, do the research with Aerobs workflows, we do the outlining again.
On Aerobs, we use like either perplexity, or we just run Google searches and scrape the results to get information from there.
And like we look for a bunch of sources, get information in a bunch of sources, and then pull that back onto Airtable.
And from there, we push that out to chat GPT for the writing.
And generally, I tend to use like multiple chat GPT operations for the writing, one to do like a first drive,
And another one to do like fact check and quality check and like copy editing and another one to do the sort of brand voice alignment sort of thing.
Because like I said in my project, that's what I found works best.
Instead of us giving it like giving one big prompt to do the writing and the copy editing and the fact checking and the brand voice.
Just to iterate with multiple steps, I found the LLM response better to that.
So that's sort of if I'm creating new content, that's what I would do.

And some other kinds of automation I've done is one of the most recent ones I built was to like update messaging because, you know, new product versions come out and we have blogs that talk about like older versions.
So essentially, I built a workflow that looks at a URL, reads the article and checks for if it's product mentioned, what's the version mentioned.
If it's the most late, if it's the latest version, then it.

just goes on through, no changes, but like, if something is different, it figures out, but it's different, and, you know, it's a cascading thing of if statements, but, you know, it's the latest, it doesn't need to be updated.

If it's updated, it writes like a brand quality guideline, sorry, it writes a document update sort of guideline thing, because I also didn't want the AI to write the finished thing, because I did want a human to see, you know, do we, like, is this right?
Does this sound good?
Instead of just updating and pushing the new thing to live.

And then, you know, write a bunch of versions of, like, guidelines for the different products mentioned, and right at the end, bring it all together into, like, one big, one big, like, update guideline document sort of thing that we then push the writing team.

So, like, new content, content refreshes are, like, some of the more interesting things that I've worked with recently, but also I do use automation a lot to, you know, do things in a few seconds that would normally take me, like, a minute or two, like, like, some kinds of keyword research.
If I have a bunch of keywords, then assign the keywords to some sort of user persona.
Instead of going through each and assigning it, I just do my research, put it into the automation, and get ChatGP to tell me, is this a buyer, is this a researcher, whatever.
Generally, big fan of automating things like that, just saving me a few seconds here and there, and then scale that up, saves me a few hours down the line.
Yeah, that's great.

I mean, look, I think that's, you know, I think you're, you know, you're, you already know the way Marcel works, and you kind of have thought through a lot of this stuff, and that the automation processes that you're describing are certainly the right kind of thinking to bring.

But the same kind of processes, I think, apply, the ability...
To articulate, like, you know, nested if statements is the way our brain works.
It's just hard to articulate sometimes or people don't have practice articulating it.

But that's our job is like articulate those if statements so that we can break down processes in a way to like embed them into workflows, which then sit inside of pipelines and hopefully get us good results at the other end.

think that's a good chunk of it.

So generally, as long as I feel like I'm not context switching, I can generally dig in on one thing and get that done.
And I generally tend to organize tasks based on priority, whatever works.
As for quality, I am a big fan of for each workflow that we test out or that we're trialing, shoot out maybe five to ten.
Like dummy articles, test articles or whatever, scan through those.
And on average, what do I see across all of them that is good?
What that I like, what do I see across all of them that I feel like needs to be improved?
And specifically, what needs to be improved?

If I'm seeing something common that needs to be fixed across all the output, I then specifically either tweak whichever part of the workflow I think is causing that, whichever, whichever, you know, LLM step essentially is causing that.
And then either test that step or test the entire output.
Really, I think mostly it comes down to like what I'm seeing.
If it's like something super factual that I think is the problem, then that's, you know, I would know where to look.
If it's something more like qualitative that I see is the problem, I'd probably look there.

But balancing those two, again, if time is not the biggest essence and the biggest issue here, or if I see that it's like a major issue, then yes, I would probably like shoot out maybe instead of 5 to 10, maybe 10 plus test dummy articles.
It's that if it's probably, you know, like really, I do tend to play it by year based on, you know, what the time constraints look like and what the output looks like.
But generally, roughly, it's just, you know, uh, try something, see if it fixes it.
Uh, if not, you know, repeat that.
Yeah, yeah, validation is, is like super.
Important in working with LLMs, you know, they're, they do tend to stray and validators can help us to understand where they are straying and where improvements need to be done.

You know, those, it's like, it's sometimes it feels like extraneous work or work that is not as important, you know, you're like, oh, okay, I did all this work to do the thing, and then I have to check my work.
But it's, it's so helpful, I find, to, to kind of, like, understand, like, oh, where is this falling down?
Is it falling down, like, in my semantics?
Like, am I talking about it the wrong, the wrong way?
And that's why it's falling over?
Or is it like, no, no, this is exactly what I want, and it's just failing?
And it's like, okay, cool, we need to pull that process apart.
Maybe make it a two-step or three-step process versus a one.
Because LLMs just love to do things one at a time.
They really hate to do multiple things at once, you know, and they're bad at it.
So, I think that's super important, you know, validations, when it comes to, like, article quality, you know, like, I wrote validators for ours, for our, like.
Like writing guidelines applications, you know, like, okay, we've got these writing guidelines applied to this article, but then you also kind of want to go like, okay, cool, now grade your work.
Like, how'd you do, right?
And it'll often go, you know what, I did pretty bad.
You're like, cool, like, if you know, please go do it.
But it can at least help you to see, you know, and help you to get an idea.

And it happened in two ways.
One is either I, like, do the brief for the writers and then the writers would do it.
Or, you know, where I would be a bit more involved is I do my own research.
I do, like...
Like a first draft of an article, if it's a new article, it's net new content, I do the drafting and then it would usually be like some back and forth between me and the writers.
Usually just one pass does it because the workflows were pretty dialed in.
Generally, because I still believe that it was important to have like an actual human, either like look at it or like do some level of editing something before we went live with content.

So the exact workflow we had was whatever cadence we had, if it's like weekly publishing or like bi-weekly, like one cadence before, so like the previous week or two weeks before, something like that, I push content to the writers so that they can, you know, fix and do whatever.
That's like for net new content for, if it's update, if it's content updates that I needed, like I said, if it's, you know, messaging updates I need, I do everything on my own.
I'd generate whatever brief or like update guidelines needed to happen.

And that generally I found that writers generally like working on net new content a bit better, so that would be the focus, but like maybe once a quarter or like once every couple of months, whatever cadence we agreed upon, we'd have like an update week or like an update couple of weeks where that's sort of what the writers worked on.
And that sort of, I felt like struck the right-ish balance between letting the writers do what they like and also getting the deliverables.

We're using the SEMrush API for keywords right now.
We have experimented with some other stuff, but I think SEMrush is still kind of working for us at this point.

But yeah, it'll, for the keyword information and enrichment, it pulls from the SEMrush API to kind of take that core keyword, find articles that are ranking well for that keyword, and then kind of get an understanding of what those articles look like, and then uses that to help generate the article.
So we have basically two ways that we can inform the structure of the article.
One way is, as you saw in article directions, like, hey, write a listicle.
I really want it to look like this, and it'll override.
And then the other way is basically like a Jesus.
The wheel, right?
Where it says, go out, call the SEMrush API, do a SERP analysis, find the articles that rank well for this, and then write me an article like that.

Honestly, we're doing that less and less, because as we work with more sophisticated clients, we find that they have a real idea of what kind of content they want to create.
If they want glossary entries, or they want thought leadership pieces, or whatever, they don't just want anything.
know what mean?
